description: The transition to containerized shipping in the 1960s sparked intense conflict between the International Longshoremen's Association (ILA) and port operators. As automation threatened to displace thousands of manual laborers, strikes paralyzed East Coast ports, ultimately leading to the 'Mechanization and Modernization' agreements that provided severance and early retirement in exchange for allowing automation.

key_events.description: ILA launches major strike across East Coast ports opposing the introduction of standardized shipping containers.
Public frustration mounts as supply chain disruptions impact consumer goods availability during the holiday season.
Federal mediators intervene, proposing a compromise involving a royalty fee on containerized cargo to fund worker pensions.
Major union leadership accepts the Mechanization and Modernization agreement, signaling a shift toward accepting automation.

calibration_notes: This scenario serves as a benchmark for how public and labor sentiment shifts from 'total resistance' to 'negotiated acceptance' when technology creates undeniable long-term efficiency gains alongside short-term job loss.
